title: The Core Foundation
subtitle: Read any chart before you buy anything

lessons.title: The Three Market Trends
lessons.visual_caption: Vedanta (VEDL) — Weekly chart showing all three trends in one stock: uptrend, downtrend, and sideways. The yellow line is the 30-week SMA.
lessons.content:
Before you learn the four stages, you must understand that price can only ever move in three directions.

1. Uptrend: The price moves upward, creating higher peaks and higher valleys. Buyers are in control.

2. Downtrend: The price moves downward, creating lower peaks and lower valleys. Sellers are in control.

3. Sideways Trend: The price moves flat, locked inside a horizontal range. Buyers and sellers have equal strength.

Look at the Vedanta chart above. You can see all three trends in a single stock over time — labelled clearly as uptrend, downtrend, and sideways. This is what every stock does. Always. Without exception.

→ Price can only go up, down, or sideways
→ The 30-week SMA (yellow line) helps you identify which trend is active
→ Your job is to identify the trend — not predict it

lessons.title: The Chart Setup
lessons.content:
Weinstein's entire method is built on one specific chart setup. Before anything else, you must set this up correctly.

The Action: Open your charting platform (TradingView is recommended) and change the timeframe to Weekly. One bar or candle now represents exactly one week.

The Indicator: Add a 30-week Simple Moving Average (SMA) line to the chart.

The Meaning: This line is the average closing price over the last 30 weeks. It removes daily noise and reveals the true direction of the trend.

The Baseline Rule:
→ When the price is above the line, the trend is strong
→ When the price is below the line, the trend is weak

Why weekly and not daily? Daily charts are full of noise — random moves that mean nothing. The weekly chart cuts through this noise and shows you what is actually happening to a stock over months and years. This is the timeframe where stage changes become clearly visible.

Why 30 weeks? Weinstein tested many different periods. The 30-week SMA has the ideal balance — slow enough to filter noise, fast enough to respond to real trend changes. It has stood the test of time across decades of market data.

lessons.title: Support and Resistance
lessons.visual_caption: Suven Life Sciences — Daily chart showing resistance at ₹222 and support at ₹203. Watch what happens when price finally breaks above the resistance level.
lessons.content:
Support and resistance are the two most important price levels on any chart.

Support: The price floor. When a stock falls to this level it tends to stop dropping, because buyers step in where the price looks cheap. You can see the support level at ₹203 on the Suven chart above.

Resistance: The price ceiling. When a stock rises to this level it tends to stop climbing, because sellers step in to take profits. Notice the resistance at ₹222 — price touched it multiple times and turned back.

The Role-Reversal Rule — this is critical:
→ If the price breaks above resistance, that old ceiling becomes the new support floor
→ If the price breaks below support, that old floor becomes the new resistance ceiling

Look at what happened on the Suven chart: after consolidating below ₹222 for weeks, the stock broke out above resistance on massive volume. That old ₹222 resistance level then became the new support.

This role-reversal is why breakouts are so powerful — the former sellers become the new buyers defending that same level.

→ Support = price floor (buyers step in)
→ Resistance = price ceiling (sellers step in)
→ Broken resistance becomes new support
→ Broken support becomes new resistance

lessons.title: Introduction to the Four Stages
lessons.visual_caption: The Weinstein four-stage cycle with 30-week moving average. Every stock follows this pattern — Stage 1 base, Stage 2 advance, Stage 3 top, Stage 4 decline, then back to Stage 1.
lessons.content:
Stan Weinstein links the three trends into one continuous, repeating four-part life cycle. Every stock is always somewhere in this cycle.

Stage 1 — Basing Phase: Sideways trend. The 30-week line is flat and horizontal. Action: Sit idle. Do not buy.

Stage 2 — Advancing Phase: Uptrend. The 30-week line is sloping upward. Action: This is where you want to be.

Stage 3 — Topping Phase: Sideways trend again, but at a high level. The 30-week line flattens again. Action: Sell half your shares.

Stage 4 — Declining Phase: Downtrend. The 30-week line slopes downward. Action: Exit. Do not buy.

Look at the Dixon chart above. Starting from the left you can see Stage 4 (the decline with the label), then a long Stage 1 basing phase (the flat, quiet period), then Stage 2 beginning (labelled) as price breaks out and surges.

The next several modules take these four stages one at a time, in the order they occur. But first, we cover the single most important supporting concept: volume.

→ Stage 1: Sideways base — wait patiently
→ Stage 2: Uptrend — the only time to buy
→ Stage 3: Topping — prepare to exit
→ Stage 4: Downtrend — stay out completely

questions.question: On which chart timeframe and with which indicator does Weinstein's method identify the major trend?
questions.explanation: The method is built on a weekly chart combined with the 30-week SMA. This filters out daily noise and reveals the major stage a stock is in.

questions.question: What happens to a level of resistance after price decisively breaks above it?
options: It disappears from the chart entirely
It becomes the new support floor
It forces volume to drop to zero
questions.explanation: Broken resistance flips role and becomes support. Old buyers who waited now defend that level, and sellers who sold there are gone. This is the role-reversal rule.

questions.question: What are the four stages in their correct order?
questions.explanation: Every stock moves through a continuous cycle: Stage 1 basing, Stage 2 advancing, Stage 3 topping, Stage 4 declining, then back to basing.
